informal: Prove that a group of order 351 has a normal Sylow $p$-subgroup for some prime $p$ dividing its order.
lean:
theorem Dummit_Foote_exercise_4_5_15 {G : Type*} [Group G] [Fintype G]
  (hG : card G = 351) :
  ∃ (p : ℕ) (P : Sylow p G), p.Prime ∧ (p ∣ card G) ∧  P.Normal
reasoning: The Lean signature takes a finite group G with |G| = 351 and asserts the existence of a prime p dividing |G| together with a Sylow p-subgroup P of G which is normal. This matches the informal claim exactly: "a group of order 351 has a normal Sylow p-subgroup for some prime p dividing its order." The quantifier structure (∃ p, ∃ P) corresponds to "for some prime p," the divisibility condition `p ∣ card G` matches "dividing its order," and `P.Normal` for a `Sylow p G` matches "normal Sylow p-subgroup." No extra hypotheses are imposed beyond Group/Fintype (necessary to even speak of order 351), and no conclusion is weakened or strengthened.
